summary: This advice column installment covers questions about graduate school prestige versus happiness, dating struggles for physics students, navigating boring workplace conversations, dealing with anti-immigration email spam from acquaintances, and a brief exchange about conference culture. The advice consistently emphasizes practical action over overthinking, the importance of being genuinely interesting rather than self-focused, and knowing when engagement with difficult people is futile. O'Neil advocates for being a 'loudmouth' who changes boring conversations rather than suffering through them, and suggests that deeply personal grievances rarely respond to factual rebuttals.
key_points: Prestige matters more for women in academia who don't get the benefit of the doubt that men receive, but matters less outside academic circles
Self-involvement is unattractive; having opinions about the world and ideals to fix problems is what makes someone interesting and even sexy
Boring conversations persist out of habit, and people often welcome someone willing to redirect them to more interesting topics
When someone's political position stems from personal grievance rather than facts, no amount of evidence will change their mind
Sticking with an exciting advisor you trust is more important than comfort in choosing graduate programs
money_quotes: It definitely still seems true that you have to be top-notch to impress people if you're female whereas men often get the benefit of the doubt.
Unless someone is already your friend, they probably don't care about that stuff. At all.
Have ideals. Have plans to fix problems. That's interesting! That's possibly even sexy!
Joy is extremely catchy. Worst case you make some new friends.
I pretty much just barge into conversations and change the topic if I'm bored.
He's gotten it into his head that he lost his job for an abstract political reason. In doing so, he's made it incredibly personal, and no amount of factual evidence is going to change his stance.
key_insight: The best way to connect with others and influence conversations is to be genuinely engaged with the world rather than focused on yourself, and to know when changing someone's mind is impossible.
